name: B.S. in Data Science

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 8-term plan is a model of ordered progression: a programming spine (CS 100 -> CS 113 -> CS 114 -> CS 241/CS 280 -> CS 288) runs in parallel with a math/stats track (MATH 111 -> 112 -> 244 Probability / 337 Linear Algebra -> 341 Statistical Methods II -> 478 Stat Methods in Data Science), and both converge into the data core (CS 331 Databases, DS 340, CS 370 AI, CS 375 ML, CS 482 Data Mining, CS 444 Big Data Systems, CS 450 Data Visualization) and a two-part capstone (CS 492/DS 493). Prerequisite depths climb smoothly from 0 to 7 with no abrupt jumps.
dimensions.suggestion: Convert the generic 'Data Science Elective 1/2/3' placeholders into named concentration tracks (e.g., ML systems, business analytics, bio/health data) so the upper-level path is as prescribed as the core.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is well-shaped: deepestPrereqChain=7 is appropriate for a quantitative four-year degree, and the 6 dangling references (missingCourseCount=6: GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) are GER placeholders, physics labs, and one-credit seminars rather than genuine data-science prerequisite breaks. Structural health is strong but not perfectly clean.
dimensions.suggestion: Add catalog entries or code mappings for YWCC 207/307 and the PHYS 111A/121A labs and resolve the GER 200/300 placeholders so no referenced code dangles.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the provided data; only a term-by-term plan and per-course descriptions are present. For a flagship B.S. this absence is a real catalog finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit program learning outcomes (e.g., what a graduate can model, engineer, analyze, visualize, and communicate) on the catalog page.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan does not reconcile: statedTotalCredits is null and creditReconcileDelta is therefore null, while summedTermCredits is only 105.0, well short of a typical ~120-credit B.S. The eighth term (Fourth Year, 2nd Semester) is truncated with null credits and just two items (a senior-seminar GER and one free elective), so both the stated total and the final-term accounting are incomplete.
dimensions.suggestion: State the program's total credit requirement and complete the eighth-term listing so the summed credits reconcile to a stated total (targeting the ~120-121 typical of a B.S.).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled by year and semester and the required courses are legible, but presentation is undercut by the truncated final term (null credits, only two rows), the null stated total, and repeated generic 'Data Science Elective' placeholders that leave the upper-level path visually open.
dimensions.suggestion: Complete the eighth-term rows, add a stated total, and replace bare elective placeholders with example course lists so the plan is fully legible end to end.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The required core is strong and current for data science: CS 375 Machine Learning, CS 482 Data Mining, CS 444 Big Data Systems, CS 450 Data Visualization, CS 370 AI, CS 331 Databases, and a deep statistics track (MATH 244/341/478). The main currency gaps are the absence of a dedicated deep-learning course beyond intro ML, no MLOps/data-engineering pipeline course, and no explicit generative-AI/LLM content.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a deep-learning course and an MLOps/data-engineering course so graduates can both build modern models and deploy them in production.

descriptionQuality.justification: Course descriptions are present and substantive across the program's spine (CS 375 Machine Learning, CS 444 Big Data Systems, CS 482 Data Mining, MATH 478 Statistical Methods in Data Science), with no thin or placeholder excerpts observed among sampled courses.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with clear course logic and grade conditions, and the only dangling references (missingCourseCount=6) are non-core GER placeholders, physics labs, and one-credit seminars rather than data-science course breaks, so the core prerequisite chain is unambiguous.

missingTopics.rationale: CS 375 is 'Introduction to Machine Learning' and CS 370 is intro AI, but there is no dedicated deep-learning course (CNNs, RNNs, transformers), which is now foundational to contemporary data science.

CS 444 Big Data Systems is infrastructure-adjacent, but no course covers data pipelines, feature stores, model deployment, or monitoring, the single biggest skills gap for industry data-science hiring.
The stats spine (MATH 244/341/478) is strong on probability and regression but has no A/B testing or causal inference, which are central to product and industry analytics.
CS 482 Data Mining and CS 370 AI touch adjacent ground, but there is no text/NLP or computer-vision course even though most modern data is unstructured.

missingTopics.topic: Responsible AI: fairness, privacy, and interpretability
missingTopics.rationale: IS 350 Computers, Society and Ethics is a general ethics course; given the ML and data-mining core, the program should add dedicated algorithmic-fairness, privacy (e.g., differential privacy), and model-interpretability content.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI and large language models
emergingTrends.rationale: The AI/ML spine (CS 370/375/482) predates the foundation-model era; a course or module on LLMs, prompting, retrieval-augmented generation, and fine-tuning is essential positioning for graduates.
Industry has shifted from model-building to reliable ML systems; CS 444 and the CS 492/DS 493 capstone could anchor a production-ML track.
CS 444 Big Data Systems should modernize toward streaming (Kafka), lakehouse architectures, and cloud data platforms that dominate current data engineering.
Regulation such as the EU AI Act and practices like model cards are arriving; graduates need fairness, privacy, and governance literacy beyond the single IS 350 ethics course.

The elective pool already includes BME 210 and BNFO 135 (bio/health signals and bioinformatics); deepening domain tracks (health, business analytics) via the elective placeholders would sharpen employability.

topSuggestions: State a total credit requirement and complete the truncated eighth term so the plan reconciles (current summed credits are only 105 against a null stated total).
Add a deep-learning course and an MLOps/data-engineering course to close the two clearest gaps between the curriculum and industry data-science practice.
Introduce generative-AI/LLM content and a responsible-AI (fairness/privacy/interpretability) offering to modernize the AI-ML core beyond IS 350's general ethics.
Convert the 'Data Science Elective 1/2/3' placeholders into named concentration tracks with example course lists.
Publish program-level learning outcomes and resolve the 6 dangling GER/lab/seminar references so the catalog page is complete.
